content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
1102 Q Street • Suite 3000 • Sacramento, CA 95811 
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886 
Dorine Martirosian 
Senior Assistant City Attorney 
Office of the Glendale City Attorney 
613 E. Broadway, Suite 220 
Glendale, CA 91206 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-18-241 
Dear Ms. Martirosian: 
November 13, 2018 
This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of Glendale City Councilmember 
Ara Najarian regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act"). 1 
Please note that we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and 
not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or 
Section 1090. Also note that we are not a finder of fact when rendering advice (In re Oglesby 
(197 5) 1 FPPC Ops. 71 ), and any advice we provide assumes your facts are complete and accurate. 
If this is not the case or if the facts underlying these decisions should change, you should contact us 
for additional advice. 
QUESTION 
Does the Act prohibit Councilmember Najarian from taking part in governmental decisions 
relating to a proposed rent stabilization ordinance given that the Councilmember has financial 
interests in businesses that own multifamily residential rental properties within th_e City? 
CONCLUSION 
Yes. The Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions because they 
would have a reasonably foreseeable material financial effect on each of those business interests 
distinguishable from those decisions' effect on the public generally. 
1 The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory 
references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices 
Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All 
regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated. 


FACTS AS PROVIDED BY REQUESTOR 
File No. A-18-241 
Page No. 2 
You are a Senior Assistant City Attorney for the City of Glendale and the authorized 
representative of Glendale City Councilmember Ara Najarian. The City will soon consider whether 
to adopt a proposed rent stabilization ordinance (the "Ordinance"), which may include either or 
both an interim rent control measure and a permanent rent measure. 
The Costa-Hawkins Rental Housing Act prohibits a municipal rent control ordinance from 
applying to single-family homes, condominiums, and residential rental units built after February 1, 
1995. (Civil Code Section 1954.50 et seq.) Accordingly, the Ordinance's rent control measures 
would only apply to multifamily residential rental units within the City built before that date. 
The Councilmember has a 33-percent ownership interest in Everett Apartments LLC, which 
owns a 15-unit multifamily residential rental property within the City, and a 20-percent ownership 
interest in Franklin Court LLC, which owns a 5-unit multifamily residential rental property within 
the City. Both those multifamily residential rental properties would be subject to the Ordinance. 
The Councilmember's respective investments in Everett Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC 
are each worth more than $2,000, the Councilmember receives income aggregating $500 or more in 
a 12-month period from each LLC, and each LLC's multifamily residential rental property at issue 
was built before February 1, 1995. 
The City asserts that the decisions relating to the Ordinance will affect more than 25 percent 
of the 71,132 total residential dwelling units within the City. The Costa-Hawkins Rental Housing 
Act would prevent the Ordinance from applying to 38,281 of those units because they are either 
• single-family homes, condominiums, or were built after February 1, 1995. Accordingly, the 
Ordinance may potentially apply to a maximum of 32,851 multifamily residential rental units 
within the City. 
ANALYSIS 
The Act's conflict of interest provisions ensure that public officials will perform their duties 
in an impartial manner, free from bias caused by their own financial interests or the interests of 
persons who have supported them. (Section 81001(b).) Toward that end, the Act prohibits an 
official from making, participating in making, or attempting to use his or her official position to 
influence a governmental decision if that decision will have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on one or more of the official's interests distinguishable from its effect on the public 
generally. (Sections 87100 and 87103.) An official's interests that may give rise to a disqualifying 
conflict of interest under the Act are set forth in Section 87103. With respect to the decisions 
relating to the Ordinance, the facts presented indicate Councilmember Najarian has the following 
interests: 
• 
Business interests in Everett Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC because the 
Councilmember has an investment of at least $2,000 in each of those LLCs. (Section 87103(a).) 


File No. A-18-241 
Page No. 3 
• 
Real property interests in the multifamily residential rental properties owned by Everett 
Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC because the Councilmember has an ownership 
interest of IO-percent or greater in each of those LLCs. (Section 87103(b); Section 82033.) 
• 
Source of income interests in Everett Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC because the 
Councilmember will receive income aggregating $500 or more from each of those LLCs in the 
12 months prior to the decisions, and in any tenant of either of those LLCs if the 
Councilmember's pro rata share of the income from the tenant aggregates $500 in the 12 
months prior to the decisions. (Section 87103(c); Section 82030(a).) 
• 
An interest in the Councilmember' s personal finances and those of immediate family members. 
(See Section 87103.) An official always has an interest in his or her personal finances. 
Foreseeability and Materiality 
Regulation 18701(a) provides that: a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the official's interest is "explicitly involved" in 
the decision; an official's interest is explicitly involved if the interest is a named party in, or the 
subject of, the decision; and an interest is the subject of a proceeding if the decision at issue 
involves the issuance, renewal, approval, denial or revocation of any license, permit, or other 
entitlement to, or contract with, the interest. 
Regulation 18701 (b) sets forth the foreseeability standard applicable to an official's interest 
that is not explicitly involved in the decision, and provides that the effect of the decision at issue on 
an official's inexplicitly involved interest is reasonably foreseeable if it "can be recognized as a 
realistic possibility and more than hypothetical or theoretical." 
None of the Councilmember's interests are a named party in or the subject of the decisions 
relating to the Ordinance. Generally, a rental property or business is not the subject of a decision 
involving rent control ordinance. (See Condotti Advice Letter, No. A-17-288, citing Gelb Advice 
Letter, No. A-91-523.) Therefore, the Councilmember's interests are not "explicitly involved" in 
those decisions. 
Real Property Interests, Source of Income Interests, and Interest in Personal Finances 
Different materiality standards apply to a decision's financial effect on an official's financial 
interest depending on the type of interest at issue. For instance, Regulation 18702.2 sets forth the 
standards applicable to the effect on a real property interest, Regulation 18702.3 provides the 
standards applicable to the effect on a source of income interest, and Regulation 18702.5 provides 
the standard applicable to the effect on an official's personal finances. 
If the decision at issue also affects an official's business interest, however, those regulations 
provide that materiality is determined pursuant to Regulation 18702.1, which sets forth the 
materiality standards applicable to a decision's effect on an official's business interest. (Regulation 
18702.2(a)(8); Regulation 18702.3(a)(4); Regulation 18702.5(c), respectively.) Therefore, we apply 
Regulation 18702.1 to determine whether the effect of decisions relating to the Ordinance would 
have a disqualifying effect on the Councilmember's financial interests. 


Business Interests in Everett Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC 
File No. A-18-241 
Page No. 4 
Regulation 18702.l(b) provides that the effect of the decision at issue is material if a 
prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably foreseeable that it would 
contribute to a change in the value of the business, and provides examples of types of decisions that 
may have a disqualifying effect under that materiality standard. Included among those examples are 
decisions which would "[a]uthorize, prohibit, regulate or otherwise establish conditions for an 
activity in which the business entity is engaged." (Regulation 18702.l(b)(l).) 
The facts presented indicate that the Ordinance may include either or both an interim rent 
control measure and a permanent rent control measure, and those measures would regulate the 
rental of multifamily residential rental units within the City built before February 1, 1995. Because 
the Ordinance would "regulate or otherwise establish conditions" for an activity in which Everett 
Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC are engaged, the rental of multifamily residential rental 
units within the City, a prudent person with sufficient information would find it reasonably 
foreseeable that the decisions relating to the Ordinance would contribute to a change in the value of 
each of those LLCs. Therefore, those decisions would have a reasonably foreseeable material 
financial effect on the Councilmember's business interests in those LLCs, 
"Public Generally" Exception 
Regulation 18703(a) sets forth the "public generally" exception, and provides in pertinent 
part as follows: 
A governmental decision's financial effect on a public official's financial 
interest is indistinguishable from its effect on the public generally if the 
official establishes that a significant segment of the public is affected and the 
effect on his or her financial interest is not unique compared to the effect on 
the significant segment. 
Thus, in order to invoke the "public generally" exception, an official must establish that the 
decision at issue affects a significant segment of the public and that the decision's effect on the 
official's interest is not unique. 
We must first determine which significant segment standard applies to the decisions relating 
to the Ordinance. For purposes of the "public generally" exception, Regulation 18703(b) defines a 
"significant segment of the public" as 25 percent of: 
(1) All businesses or non-profit entities within the official's jurisdiction; 
(2) All real property, commercial real property, or residential real property 
within the official's jurisdiction; 
(3) All individuals within the official's jurisdiction. 


File No. A-18-241 
Page No. 5 
Because the Ordinance would generally apply to multifamily residential rental properties built 
before February 1, 1995, including Everett Apartments LLC and Franklin Court LLC's multifamily 
residential rental properties, and because the Councilmember's real property interests in those 
LLCs' multifamily residential rental properties are related to all of the Councilmember's interests at 
issue, the applicable significant segment is 25 percent of all residential real property within the City. 
The City asserts that the decisions relating to the Ordinance will affect more than 25 percent 
of the 71,132 total residential dwelling units within the City. The Costa-Hawkins Rental Housing 
Act would prevent the Ordinance from applying to 38,281 of those units because they are either 
single-family homes, condominiums, or were built after February 1, 1995. Therefore, the Ordinance 
may potentially apply to a maximum of 32,851 multifamily residential rental units, approximately 
46 percent of the 71,132 total residential dwelling units within the City. Because the Ordinance may 
apply to 46 percent of the residential real property within the City, the decisions relating to the 
Ordinance would affect a significant segment of public under Regulation l 8703(b )(2). 
We next ·consider whether the decisions related to the Ordinance would have a unique effect 
on one or more of the Councilmember's financial interests. Regulation 18703(c) provides in 
pertinent part as follows: 
(c) A unique effect on a public official's financial interest includes a 
disproportionate effect on: 
[i!] ... [i!] 
(3) An official's interests in business entities or real properties resulting from 
the cumulative effect of the official's multiple interests in similar entities or 
properties that is substantially greater than the effect on a single interest. 
The Councilmember has substantial investments in two residential rental property 
businesses: a 33-percent ownership interest in Everett Apartments LLC, which owns a 15-unit 
multifamily residential rental property within the City, and a 20-percent ownership interest in 
Franklin Court LLC, which owns a 5-unit multifamily residential rental property within the City. 
Moreover, the Ordinance may apply to both of those properties, and have considerable effects on 
the residential rental properties within the City. 
Because the potential cumulative effect of decisions relating to the Ordinance on the 
Councilmember's multiple interests related to the LLCs' multifamily residential rental properties 
would be substantially greater than those decisions' effect on a single residential rental unit, the 
decisions related to the Ordinance would have a unique effect on the Councilmember' s interests. 
Therefore, the Councilmember may not invoke the "public generally" exception with respect to the 
decisions related to the Ordinance. 
Thus, the Act prohibits the Councilmember from taking part in those decisions. 


File No. A-18-241 
Page No. 6 
If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660. 
MFC:gal 
Sincerely, 
Dave Bainbridge 
General Counsel 
Ulll-~ 
By: 
Matthew F. Christy 
Counsel, Legal Division
